text: The thing about math is that after you do all the crunching, you need to apply sanity to validate that your answer is correct. If your crunching says the ball travelled at the speed of light when you dropped it off a building, you did the math wrong. If your model says one of the best players on a leading basketball team was one of the least productive on that same team, the model is wrong. The model is aimed at predicting the future, to some extent, and you correct it by comparing its predictions to the actual; not blindly following it.
label: analysis

text: This is just the danger of people having access to information they don't understand or know how to use. Numbers don't lie, but people sure do. If anyone actually thinks JB makes a team worse, they should be fully ignored. Just extremely dumb shit. And I day this as someone that finds JB insufferable.
label: hot_take

text: Yup, we are witnessing in real time how statistically illiterate many people are. The statistics aren’t wrong, they can only show what they are created and calibrated to show, in that they have succeeded. How we interpret the numbers a statistical model spits out is up to us. And that’s the problem, most people just see a statistic and think higher number = good and lower = bad, but all statistics require context to decipher. There are too many confounding variables that you can’t necessarily control for that can muddy a statistic for any given player. Furthermore, you have to know how a statistic is calculated. A lot of these models use weird extrapolations of data to get their result
label: analysis

text: The problem with plus/minus is that people treat it like it's an advanced stat or like it's a true aggregate stat when it really is just like a base stat. It actually should be treated more like a counting stat. It's the counting stat of advanced stats. Your particular plus minus might not tell me much but the difference between plus minuses, identifying changes and relationships etc, can tell me a ton. Can get me like 80% there

text: Thats why what actually matters should be points relative to the general trend line. text: I mean, at that point it just boils down to points per 100 possessions on/off, right? And for Brown it's 120.809(on)/121.164(off), so -0.35. That doesn't really explain if he's making his teammates better or worse and it helps there to use point per possession: Brown's ppp 1.165 Teammates ppp 1.232 (Brown on) Teammates ppp 1.211 (Brown off) So, there is a +1.68% lift for his teammates in their ppp, but because Browns is lower, points per 100 possessions goes down (Brown's usage is 36.2%, so (36.21.165)+(63.81.232)=120.8) For a comparison using SGA, his on/off is 122.916/113.740, for +9.8 and for ppp: SGA ppp 1.356 Teammates ppp 1.165 (SGA on) Teammates ppp 1.137 (SGA off) There is a lift in teammates ppp but because he's do much more efficient, there's a big lift in points per 100 possessions. And hypothetically, if you swapped Brown and SGA on OKC and the teammate ppp stays the same, Brown would have a positive on/off (116.5/113.7, +2.8). Average points per 100 is 115.7, so Brown is above average, it's just that the rest of his team is way above average so he brings it down.
label: analysis
notes: Clear analysis: load-bearing on/off + ppp numbers with reasoning.
